title: How AI Learns: You Teach It!

modules.title: The Machine That Watches and Learns
modules.sub: Discover how a robot picks up habits from watching you
modules.description: Learners follow a classroom robot as it quietly observes the kids around it and starts copying their behaviors. Students make choices about which actions the robot gets to watch, then see how those observations shape what the robot begins to do on its own.

modules.title: Feeding an AI: Data Is Its Food
modules.sub: Choose what examples an AI eats to grow smarter
modules.description: Students explore the idea that data is the fuel an AI runs on, deciding which examples to feed the classroom robot from a mixed pile of good and silly inputs. They immediately see how their choices change the robot's behavior, building an intuition for why the quality of data matters.

modules.title: What Happens When Data Is Wrong?
modules.sub: Track down the bad examples causing the robot's strange mistakes
modules.description: Learners investigate why the robot has developed odd and unhelpful habits by hunting through the training examples for errors, bias, and weird outliers. They decide which bad data to remove or replace, then observe how cleaning up the data steers the robot back toward sensible behavior.

modules.title: Train Your Own Sorting Machine
modules.sub: Build and label a real training set to teach a sorter
modules.description: Students take on the role of AI trainer, creating and labeling their own set of examples to teach a simple sorting machine how to group objects correctly. They test their finished training set against new examples and reflect on what made their labeled data strong or where it still needs improvement.

synopsis: Join a cast of kids who accidentally teach a classroom robot some very strange habits, then race to fix it by understanding how machines actually learn from examples. You will make real decisions about what data to feed an AI and see the results immediately in the story.
rationale: Children aged 8-10 interact daily with recommendation systems and smart devices but have no mental model for machine learning, leaving them unable to question why an AI behaves the way it does.
learning_outcome: Students can explain in their own words that AI learns from data examples, demonstrate how bad data leads to bad results, and produce a labeled training set for a simple sorting task.
